items.title: The US-China tug of war over Panama | Mapped Out
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The Panama Canal puts Panama on the map – but has also set it at the center of the US-China rivalry in Latin America. US President Donald Trump says he wants to "take back" the canal and his administration has pressured Panama to counter growing Chinese influence. The US military is back in the country and business deals involving Blackrock to get an interest in Chinese-linked ports are up in the air. In this Mapped Out episode we spoke to experts from the US, China and Panama. So, where is Panama heading in this geopolitical powerplay?  


00:00 Why the US & China have their sights on the Panama Canal  
02:53 Building the canal: the controversial US legacy in Panama  
05:58 Panama's sudden ditching of Taiwan  
08:41 Chinese-linked ports: when business deals get political  
10:25 Bowing to US pressure?  
12:06 US or China: Where is Panama leaning? 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: South Korean workers detained by ICE at Hyundai plant return home | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Some 300 South Korean workers have returned home after an ICE raid at a Hyundai plant in Atlanta, Georgia, which sparked massive condemnation of the US in South Korea. 
While Seoul said President Donald Trump offered to let the workers stay to train Americans, only one person took up the offer.

items.title: Why have student protests in Nepal died down after days of violent unrest? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Calm has returned to Nepal with the instalment of a new interim Prime Minister Sushila Karki , after the previous leadership was toppled in anti-government protests. Karki is a former supreme court chief justice and Nepal's first female leader. She will lead a six month transition to elections, scheduled for March 6th. Dozens were killed in the unrest that erupted earlier this week over corruption and the government's decision to restrict social media. 

DW Correspondent Adil Bhat reports from Kathmandu

#nepalprotests #genznepal #sushilakarki

items.title: Are large corporations hindering the full potential of genetic engineering? | Transforming Business
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Genetically modified food and seeds are under fire. Yet scientists agree: genetic engineering is a key technology for boosting agricultural yields and profits. It can also contribute to environmental and climate protection—through drought-resistant crops, for example, like those developed by the Argentine company Bioceres. New methods such as genome editing and CRISPR/Cas9 are expanding the possibilities. While North and South America have long embraced genetic engineering, skepticism remains high in Europe and Asia. In the EU, green genetic engineering is effectively banned, and genetically modified organisms (GMOs) are strictly regulated in many countries. That makes approval for new products expensive and time-consuming—often only affordable for major corporations like Bayer, Syngenta, or BASF. These companies face criticism for dominating the market. NGOs like Greenpeace also play a role in shaping the negative image of green genetic engineering. This episode of Transforming Business explores the potential of genetic engineering in agriculture—and asks whether the criticism of companies like Bayer & Co is truly justified.

Chapters
0:00 Intro
0:13 What is genetic engineering?  
1:43 The potential of genetic engineering  
5:04 Why does genetic engineering have such a bad reputation?  
7:45 Genetic engineering and climate protection  
9:53 The market power of corporations  
11:42 Legal regulations  
13:39 Public perception and the role of NGOs  
16:48 Outro
 
#TransformingBusiness #GMO #Agriculture

items.title: German cabinet agrees to boost army recruitment | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany's cabinet has recently passed a draft law to introduce voluntary military service. The legislation leaves the door open for a return to compulsory conscription, which was suspended in 2011. As the army, or Bundeswehr, struggles to fill the ranks, we asked young Germans how they feel about the plan.

items.title: Tackling Germany's skilled labor shortage | Focus on Europe
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany has a significant skilled labor shortage, with millions of unfilled vacancies despite a large pool of unemployed people, as the unemployed often lack the specific qualifications needed.  Germany is also seeking skilled workers in the logistics sector. Truck drivers are now being trained in Burkina Faso and Côte d’Ivoire.

items.title: Military plan aims to recruit 80,000 new troops | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>In the face of the threat posed by Russia, Germany is on a military mission of its own — to recruit tens of thousands of troops. The country is calling on young Germans to fill those vacancies. The German cabinet has approved a draft plan for voluntary military service, which aims to increase troop numbers by 80,000 over the coming years, for a total of 260,000. 
The plan, which still requires parliamentary approval, involves sending an online questionnaire to all 18-year-olds, asking if they are willing to volunteer for military service. Men would be required to complete the form, but women would not.  Then, the army will invite suitable candidates to attend basic training. Although the plan is currently voluntary, the government isn't ruling out compulsory conscription if not enough people sign up. 

Chapters:
0:00 Young Germans asked to boost military ranks
1:23 Jakob Blasel, Head of Germany's Young Greens & Michael Bayer, Germany's Young Conservatives

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Poland has closed its border to Belarus, and Lithuania has boosted patrols | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Russia and Belarus have launched joint military drills, which some of their neighbors are watching nervously. The exercises, called Zapad 2025, will run until Tuesday. The drills come just days after a Russian drone incursion into Polish airspace, which has stoked already heightened NATO concerns about the threat from Russia. The Kremlin has dismissed fears over the exercises as "emotional overload." However, Moscow did use similar drills to launch its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022. 

Chapters:
0:00 Russia, Belarus conduct joint military drills
0:35 Ryhor Astapenia, Belarus Initiative at Chatham House
8:22 Natalia Smolentceva DW Correspondent

items.title: How are the Taliban's strict restrictions on women's situation in this disaster? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Nearly two weeks after an earthquake struck eastern Afghanistan, leaving parts of the region in ruins, thousands of people remain without shelter. They live in constant fear of landslides and aftershocks. The earthquake killed at least 2,200 people. Nearly 7,000 homes were destroyed. The World Health Organization has asked the Taliban to lift restrictions on female aid workers in order to provide better assistance to women who are struggling to access necessary care.  

Chapters:
0:00 Afghan quake victims still struggling to access aid
3:10 Kiana Alavi | Norwegian Refugee Council
7:40 Mihyung Park | International Organization for Migration
